scope: Stationarity-oriented features (differencing, explicitly modeling trend and repeating patterns).
key_concepts: Many machine learning algorithms, especially tree-based models, struggle to extrapolate outside the numerical range seen during training. A growing trend breaks this assumption.
Differencing transforms a non-stationary series (where the mean changes over time) into a stationary one by taking the difference between consecutive observations.
By predicting the change instead of the absolute value, models can handle overall trends more safely.
Trend features can also be captured explicitly, such as by calculating the linear slope over a rolling window.
Seasonality involves repeating patterns at fixed intervals. Seasonal differencing subtracts the value from the same point in the previous cycle.
Explicit seasonal features (like day-of-week or month-of-year) allow models to learn periodic effects directly.

definitions.meaning: The process of computing the difference between consecutive observations to remove trends.
A state where the statistical properties of a time series, like mean and variance, are constant over time.
Predictable and recurring periodic fluctuations in a time series.

examples: Instead of predicting cumulative monthly sales that grow every year, predicting the month-over-month change in sales.

relations.to_ch06_s06: Relates to the date/time and cyclical features discussed in Chapter 6.

exclusions: Formal statistical tests for stationarity like Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) tests.
